description: Fragrant, fringed-petal flowers that bring a classic, cottage-garden feel to any balcony. Their spicy, clove-like scent is delightful, and many varieties are surprisingly hardy. The petals are edible and make a beautiful garnish.

characteristics.soilType: Well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral potting compost

care.sowIndoors: Sow seeds thinly on the surface of moist seed compost, lightly cover with vermiculite. Keep at 15-20°C. Germination in 14-21 days.
care.sowOutdoors: Sow seeds thinly in situ once the soil has warmed up in late spring. Thin to 20cm apart.
care.hardenOff: Gradually expose indoor-raised plants to outdoor conditions over 7-10 days before planting out.
care.plantOut: Plant in a sunny, well-drained spot. Don't bury the stems too deep. Pinks hate waterlogged roots.
care.watering: Water regularly until established, then only when the soil surface is dry. Avoid overhead watering.
care.feeding: Feed with a high-potash liquid feed every 2 weeks during the flowering season to encourage more blooms.
care.harvest: Pick flowers as they open for indoor display or for edible petals. Remove the bitter white base of the petals before eating.
care.overwintering: Cut back after flowering. Most garden pinks are hardy and will survive a London winter if well-drained.

propagation.seedDescription: Small, flat, black or dark brown seeds.
propagation.cuttingsMethod: Take 5-10cm non-flowering shoots in summer, root in moist, gritty compost.
propagation.divisionMethod: Divide large, established clumps in spring or autumn.

deadheading.info: Moderately remove spent flowers down to the base of the stem to encourage continuous blooming.

edible.info: The petals have a mild, spicy, clove-like flavor. Only eat the petals; the white base is bitter.

seedSaving.method: Allow some flower heads to dry on the plant. Once the seed heads turn brown and brittle, collect the small black seeds.
seedSaving.seedPodDescription: Small, capsule-like pods that open at the top when ripe.

funFact: The name 'Dianthus' comes from the Greek words 'dios' (god) and 'anthos' (flower), meaning 'Flower of the Gods'!
